articles.title: Propensity Score-Matched Comparison of Patient-Reported Outcomes Between Crowe I Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip and Primary Osteoarthritis Following Total Hip Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Saluja A, Eschert J, Ren R, Wong Z, Su E
articles.population: Patients undergoing total hip arthroplasty for either Crowe I developmental dysplasia of the hip or primary osteoarthritis
articles.sample_size: 210 hips (105 with developmental dysplasia of the hip, 105 with osteoarthritis)
articles.outcomes: Modified Harris Hip Score, Hip disability and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score - Joint Replacement, pain scores, activity levels, revision rates, and implant survivorship
articles.take_home: Patients with Crowe I developmental dysplasia of the hip achieved similar improvements in pain, function and activity after hip replacement compared to those with osteoarthritis, with comparable implant survival rates and revision rates. A patient's functional ability before surgery, rather than their diagnosis, was the main factor determining how much they benefited from the operation.

articles.title: What Factors Increase the Risk of Dislocation after Primary Total Hip Arthroplasty Using the Direct Anterior Approach?
articles.authors: Murphy MP, Forti CR, Ho H, Hopper RH Jr, Hamilton WG
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study with matched case-control analysis
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip arthroplasty via direct anterior approach
articles.outcomes: Dislocation rate and associated risk factors including patient demographics, implant characteristics, and radiographic parameters such as cup orientation, offset, and limb length changes
articles.take_home: Hip dislocation after direct anterior approach surgery is uncommon at 0.5%, but women with smaller femoral head implants (28mm or 32mm) have significantly higher risk. Precise positioning of the cup during surgery is important to reduce dislocation risk.

articles.title: Robotic Total Hip Arthroplasty in Atypical Hip Anatomy: Accuracy of Component Positioning and Clinical Outcomes in 192 Complex Cases.
articles.authors: Kayani B, Enson J, Donaldson J, Miles J, Newman S, Jayadev C, et al.
articles.population: Patients with atypical hip anatomy undergoing robotic total hip arthroplasty, including developmental dysplasia, Perthes disease, slipped capital femoral epiphysis, previous acetabular fracture, and skeletal dysplasia
articles.outcomes: Component positioning accuracy, patient satisfaction, functional scores (UCLA activity, WOMAC, Oxford Hip Score, Forgotten Joint Score), component survivorship, and complications
articles.take_home: Robotic-assisted hip replacement achieved highly accurate component positioning in patients with complex hip anatomy, with 98.8% implants surviving to five years and patients reporting high satisfaction and good functional outcomes. This suggests robotic assistance may help achieve better results in this challenging patient group.

articles.title: Social Determinants of Health Z-Codes are Associated with Low Compliance with Patient-Reported Outcome Measures after Total Hip Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Parry M, Ozdag Y, Kloc A, Salvato J, Mercuri JJ
articles.population: Patients undergoing elective total hip arthroplasty between 2017 and 2022
articles.outcomes: Compliance with patient-reported outcome measures at two years after surgery; overall survey completion rate was 25.8%
articles.take_home: Only about one quarter of hip replacement patients completed outcome questionnaires at two years after surgery. Patients with social disadvantage codes, those who did not speak English, and men were less likely to complete questionnaires, whilst married or employed patients were more likely to do so.

articles.title: Differences Between Inpatient and Outpatient Medicare Total Knee Arthroplasty: Substantial Clinical Benefit Thresholds on Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score for Joint Replacement.
articles.authors: Patel SV, Pasqualini I, Khan ST, Elmenawi KA, Zhang C, Piuzzi NS
articles.study_type: Retrospective analysis of prospectively collected data
articles.population: Medicare beneficiaries aged 65 and over who underwent primary elective total knee replacement
articles.outcomes: Knee injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score for Joint Replacement (KOOS-JR) substantial clinical benefit thresholds
articles.take_home: Inpatients needed a 29.3-point improvement on the KOOS-JR score to show meaningful benefit, whilst outpatients needed only a 21.5-point improvement. This suggests that using different success thresholds for hospital and outpatient surgery settings may be fairer than applying a single benchmark to both groups.

articles.title: Risk and Benefit Profile with Concurrent Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs and Direct Oral Anticoagulants Following Total Knee Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Paul BR, Verhey JT, Haak GM, Braithwaite CL, Tarabichi S, Christopher ZK, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using national claims database
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total knee arthroplasty who received direct oral anticoagulants for blood clot prevention
articles.sample_size: 12,733 patients receiving NSAIDs plus DOAC; 37,127 receiving DOAC alone
articles.outcomes: Wound complications, surgical site infection, periprosthetic joint infection, revision surgery, arthrofibrosis, and bleeding events at 90 days and 2 years
articles.take_home: Taking NSAIDs together with blood-thinning medications after knee replacement increases the risk of wound problems, infection, and needing revision surgery, although it does reduce stiffness. Doctors need to carefully consider each patient's individual risks when deciding whether to use NSAIDs with these blood thinners.

articles.title: Complications of Ceramic-on-Ceramic Total Hip Arthroplasty with Large Heads: 10-Year Minimum Follow-Up.
articles.authors: Lee DH, Park JW, Jung WH, Kim TY, Ha YC, Koo KH, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing cementless total hip replacement with ceramic-on-ceramic bearings
articles.sample_size: 178 patients (201 hips) followed for minimum 10 years
articles.outcomes: Ceramic fracture, dislocation, Harris Hip Score, osteolysis, articular noise, revision and reoperation rates
articles.take_home: Large ceramic hip replacements did not fracture and had excellent survival rates at 10+ years follow-up, but a small number of patients experienced late dislocation due to spine problems and some reported noise from the joint.

articles.title: Disparities in Preoperative Weight Loss and Obesity Treatment Before Total Joint Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Seward MW, Grimm JA, Bedard NA, Hannon CP, Berry DJ, Abdel MP
articles.population: Patients undergoing total hip or knee replacement with BMI of 30 or above
articles.sample_size: 6,128 patients (4,953 with socioeconomic data) from 44,764 joint replacements
articles.outcomes: Preoperative obesity treatment utilization (weight loss medications, nutrition services, bariatric surgery) and weight loss success; disparities by socioeconomic status and sex
articles.take_home: Very few patients received obesity treatment before joint replacement surgery, with less than 3% receiving any intervention. Women and patients from more deprived areas were more likely to have higher BMI before surgery but were less likely to achieve weight loss.

articles.title: Construction of a prediction model for isolated distal deep vein thrombosis after total knee arthroplasty based on machine learning.
articles.authors: Chen JH, Zhang ZF, Guo SH
articles.study_type: Prediction model development study using machine learning
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Incidence of isolated distal deep vein thrombosis (IDDVT) within three days after surgery; predictive model performance measured by AUC scores
articles.take_home: About 15 out of every 100 patients developed blood clots in the deep veins of the leg after knee replacement surgery. Researchers created a prediction tool using seven key factors that can help identify which patients are at higher risk of developing these clots, so they can receive preventive treatment earlier.

articles.title: Evaluation of the performance of large language models in the management of hip and knee osteoarthritis: analysis of EULAR 2023 recommendations.
articles.authors: Mirza Y, Sari F, Arslan S
articles.population: Large language models (DeepSeek-R1, Gemini 2.5 Flash, ChatGPT-5)
articles.sample_size: 8 EULAR 2023 recommendations converted into open-ended questions
articles.outcomes: Informational quality, reliability, usability, readability, semantic and lexical similarity to EULAR guidelines
articles.take_home: DeepSeek-R1 performed better than other models at answering questions about hip and knee osteoarthritis management, but all three models produced generally acceptable information. However, these artificial intelligence tools are not yet ready for patients to use independently and should only be used as supportive tools under healthcare professional supervision.

articles.title: A System in Motion: The Evolution of Orthopaedic Care in Romania.
articles.authors: Dragosloveanu S, Nedelea DG, Scheau C
articles.population: Romanian orthopaedic patients
articles.outcomes: Digital infrastructure adoption, robotic-assisted surgery use, artificial intelligence applications, hospital bed capacity, arthroplasty register modernisation
articles.take_home: Romania's orthopaedic care is advancing through increased use of digital tools and robotic surgery, whilst maintaining high hospital bed capacity for elderly and complex patients. The country has relaunched its joint replacement register to match standards across Europe.

articles.title: Salvage total hip arthroplasty after internal fixation compared with acute total hip arthroplasty for fracture: a cohort study based on 32,960 cases from the Dutch Arthroplasty Register.
articles.authors: Van Marle L, Peters RM, Van Steenbergen LN, Jaarsma RL, Doornberg JN, Zijlstra WP
articles.study_type: Cohort study using national register data
articles.population: Patients who had total hip replacement for proximal femur fracture, either as initial treatment or after failed internal fixation
articles.sample_size: 32,960 procedures (12,486 salvage after failed fixation, 18,450 acute fracture replacements)
articles.outcomes: Need for revision hip replacement surgery within 1 and 5 years, and reasons for revision
articles.take_home: Patients who needed hip replacement after failed internal fixation had similar revision rates to those who had hip replacement straight away for their fracture. Both groups had revision rates around 4.5% at 5 years, suggesting that previous internal fixation does not make the later hip replacement less successful.

articles.title: A multivariable MRI-based risk model for medial meniscal extrusion progression and treatment failure.
articles.authors: Endara Urresta F, Penaherrera-Carrillo C, Barros Castro A, Zuniga S
articles.study_type: Observational cohort study with internal validation
articles.population: Patients with medial knee pain and medial meniscal displacement on MRI
articles.outcomes: Treatment failure defined as major subsequent surgery, clinically relevant meniscal reintervention, or meaningful deterioration in patient-reported outcomes at mean follow-up of 32.4 months
articles.take_home: A new MRI-based risk model that combines measurements of meniscal displacement with other structural factors like root injury, knee alignment and cartilage damage can accurately predict which patients will need further surgery or experience poor outcomes. The model identified low, intermediate and high-risk groups with treatment failure rates of 12%, 34% and 64% respectively, helping doctors make better decisions about meniscus preservation surgery.

articles.title: Comparison of isokinetic strength recovery following ACL reconstruction among graft types.
articles.authors: Schlangen E, Wenzlaff S, Swetkovich R, Arenz A, Miller B, Murphy R, et al.
articles.population: Patients aged 16-35 years undergoing primary ACL reconstruction
articles.sample_size: 262 participants (hamstring tendon 134, quadriceps tendon 67, bone-patellar tendon-bone 27, allograft 34)
articles.outcomes: Isokinetic quadriceps and hamstring muscle strength measured at 3, 6, 9, and 12 months after surgery
articles.take_home: Different graft types used in ACL reconstruction show different patterns of strength recovery. Hamstring and allograft grafts restored quadriceps strength better than quadriceps tendon and bone-patellar tendon-bone grafts, but hamstring grafts led to greater weakness of the hamstring muscles because of damage at the donor site.

articles.title: A novel stepwise lateral patellar facetectomy technique in total knee arthroplasty: a comparative clinical and radiological study.
articles.authors: Ozer M, Ardali D, Birinci M, Kemah B, Polat O, Camur S
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 203 patients (99 in lateral patellar facetectomy group, 104 in comparison group)
articles.outcomes: Knee Society Score, Oxford score, Kujala score, patellar displacement, lateral displacement, congruence angle, and tilt angle on X-rays
articles.take_home: Patients who had a lateral patellar facetectomy (removal of part of the kneecap's outer edge) during knee replacement had better pain scores and better alignment of the kneecap on X-rays compared to those who had only bone spur removal. This technique may reduce kneecap-related problems after knee replacement.

articles.title: Replication of the Robotic Evaluation of Articular Laxity (REAL) classification using intraoperative gap data in robot-assisted total knee arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Morita Y, Mylonakis N, Shin J, El-Hassan M, Shichman I, Kenanidis E, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing robot-assisted total knee arthroplasty for osteoarthritis
articles.outcomes: Mediolateral imbalance classification using REAL framework; distribution of imbalance measurements; patient-reported outcomes across imbalance categories
articles.take_home: The study confirmed that the REAL classification system can reliably categorise knee imbalance during robot-assisted knee replacement surgery, and showed that surgical balancing during the procedure significantly reduced mediolateral imbalance. However, patient-reported outcomes did not differ based on initial imbalance category.

articles.title: 1-year patient-reported outcomes in patients with a documented GLP-1 agonist prescription history prior to total hip arthroplasty: a retrospective cohort study.
articles.authors: Benyamini B, Khan ST, Elmenawi KA, Patel SV, Deren ME, Piuzzi NS
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip replacement
articles.sample_size: 12,749 patients (145 with GLP-1 agonist prescription history)
articles.outcomes: 1-year patient-reported outcomes using HOOS Pain, Physical Function and Joint Replacement scores; patient satisfaction; 90-day readmission and medical complications; length of stay; 2-year implant complications
articles.take_home: Patients taking GLP-1 agonists before hip replacement had similar satisfaction and pain relief at 1 year compared to those not taking these drugs, but experienced more medical complications in the first 90 days and fewer hospital readmissions. The study was limited by its small number of patients taking GLP-1 agonists.

articles.title: Comparative Analysis of Immune Cell-Type Abundances in Periprosthetic Tissues Across Arthroplasty Failure Etiologies: Use of Transcriptomic Deconvolution.
articles.authors: Li Y, Wang F, Li Y, Mu W, Ji B, Zhang X, et al.
articles.study_type: Comparative observational study using transcriptomic analysis
articles.population: Patients with failed hip or knee replacements: 135 with periprosthetic joint infection and 50 with aseptic failure
articles.outcomes: Immune cell type abundances in infected versus aseptic failed joint tissues; recurrence rates of infection based on CD8+ T cell to regulatory T cell ratio
articles.take_home: Different types of immune cells are present in higher numbers in infected joint replacements compared with non-infected failures, and patients with a higher ratio of CD8+ T cells to regulatory T cells had lower rates of infection coming back. This new laboratory method could help identify which patients with infected joint replacements are at higher risk of treatment failure.

articles.title: A Proposed Alternative Sampling Strategy for Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services Arthroplasty Patient-Reported Outcome Performance Measure Compliance.
articles.authors: Lyman S, Chin A, Alschuler DM, Brill CO, Fontana M, MacLean C, et al.
articles.population: Hospitals performing total hip and knee arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: Analysis of hospitals with 5, 50, 500, and 5,000 eligible cases annually
articles.outcomes: Minimum sample size needed to achieve statistically valid patient-reported outcome performance measure estimates with 95% confidence interval
articles.take_home: The current CMS requirement for matching pre- and post-operative questionnaires from at least 50% of patients creates problems at both ends: small hospitals get unreliable results whilst large hospitals face excessive reporting burden. A random sampling approach would reduce the number of questionnaires needed, particularly for larger hospitals, whilst maintaining statistical accuracy.

articles.title: First Revision Total Hip Arthroplasty for Instability: 10-Year Outcomes and Risk Factors Associated with Re-Revision.
articles.authors: Yapp LZ, Howard LC, Greidanus NV, Garbuz DS, Masri BA, Neufeld ME
articles.population: Patients undergoing first revision total hip arthroplasty for instability
articles.outcomes: 10-year survivorship free from instability-related re-revision (82.2%) and all-cause re-revision (73.6%). Risk factors for failure included younger age, higher BMI, abductor deficiency, and constrained liner use.
articles.take_home: About one in five patients who have revision hip surgery for instability will experience the hip becoming unstable again within 10 years. Dual mobility components and larger femoral heads performed better than constrained liners in preventing recurrent instability.

articles.title: Jumbo Femoral Heads in Total Hip Arthroplasty: Improved Early Stability Without Added Complications.
articles.authors: Kim BI, Cheng R, Grabov E, Debbi EM, Vigdorchik JM, Mayman DJ, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip replacement
articles.sample_size: 6,413 procedures (5,897 with 36 mm heads, 516 with 40 or 44 mm heads)
articles.outcomes: Hip dislocation rates, periprosthetic joint infection, fracture complications
articles.take_home: Jumbo femoral heads (40 or 44 mm) showed a small reduction in hip dislocation risk during the first year after surgery compared to standard large heads (36 mm), but this benefit did not last long-term. There were no increased rates of infection or fracture with the larger heads.

articles.title: Dogma Under the Knife: Challenging the Status Quo in Primary Total Knee Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Padgett BM, Terhune EB, Chalmers BP, Pagnano MW, Hannon CP
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Patient satisfaction, implant survivorship, pain relief, functional improvement
articles.take_home: Although knee replacements are very successful overall, about 10 to 20% of patients remain unhappy after surgery even without technical problems. Moving away from rigid surgical approaches towards personalised strategies based on individual patient factors may help improve satisfaction.

articles.title: Revision Total Joint Arthroplasty at the Ambulatory Surgery Center: A Single Institutional Experience.
articles.authors: Acuna AJ, Potluri AS, Yadav AS, Alfonsi S, Forlenza EM, Della Valle CJ
articles.population: Patients undergoing aseptic revision total joint arthroplasty at an ambulatory surgery centre
articles.sample_size: 153 patients (181 enrolled, 28 excluded due to insufficient follow-up)
articles.outcomes: Complications within 90 days postoperatively, re-revision rates at final follow-up (mean 3.4 years), survivorship free from re-revision at 2 and 5 years
articles.take_home: Revision joint replacement can be safely performed at ambulatory surgery centres in carefully selected patients, with low complication rates (11% within 90 days) and good long-term outcomes (94% free from further revision at 5 years).

articles.title: The "Distal Femoral Replacement Downgrade": Technique of Using Bicondylar Femoral Cones with a Hinge Total Knee Arthroplasty Revision in Patients Who Have Massive Distal Femoral Bone Loss as an Alternative.
articles.authors: Chalmers BP, Afzal S, Bhatti P, Anatone AJ, Blevins JL, Debbi EM, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing revision total knee replacement for severe distal femoral bone loss
articles.outcomes: Survivorship free from revision for loosening (100% at one and two years); survivorship free from any re-revision (85.1% at one and two years); survivorship free from any reoperation (85.1% at one and two years)
articles.take_home: Using bone-preserving cones with a hinged knee replacement for severe femoral bone loss achieved good results, with no cases needing revision for loosening in the first two years, though three patients needed further surgery. This technique may be a useful alternative to distal femoral replacement in selected patients.

articles.title: Long-term outcomes of a cemented cruciate-retaining total knee arthroplasty: a 10-year follow-up study.
articles.authors: Kalaai S, Schotanus MGM, van Dun B, Most J, Tilman PBJ, Van Haaren EH, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing cemented cruciate-retaining total knee replacement
articles.outcomes: Implant survival rate, patient-reported outcome measures, radiographic signs, revision surgery rates
articles.take_home: At nearly 14 years follow-up, 95.4% of knee replacements were still in place without revision surgery, and patients reported continued improvement in function and outcomes. The Vanguard knee replacement system showed good safety and longevity.

articles.title: Curved decelerations result in greater frontal-plane knee loading than straight-line tasks.
articles.authors: Galmiche L, Kersting UG, Bill K
articles.population: Healthy recreationally active males
articles.outcomes: Peak external knee abduction moment, ground reaction force, frontal-plane moment arm during different deceleration tasks
articles.take_home: Decelerating whilst moving in a curved path places significantly greater stress on the knee joint compared to stopping in a straight line, even when approaching at the same speed. This may help explain why ACL injuries often happen during rapid changes of direction in sports.

articles.title: Machine learning-based clinical tool for identifying factors associated with symptomatic knee osteoarthritis: the Nagahama study.
articles.authors: Taniguchi M, Nakazato K, Ikezoe T, Tsuboyama T, Ito H, Matsuda S, et al.
articles.study_type: Machine learning model development and validation study
articles.population: Adults aged 60 years and over with radiographically confirmed knee osteoarthritis
articles.outcomes: Development of a clinical scoring tool to identify factors associated with symptomatic knee osteoarthritis; model discriminative performance measured by area under the receiver operating characteristic curve
articles.take_home: Researchers created a scoring tool that can help identify which modifiable factors are most important in determining whether someone with knee osteoarthritis has symptoms, including factors like low back pain, depression, muscle strength and movement range. The tool showed moderate accuracy in distinguishing between people with and without symptoms.

articles.title: Factors associated with substantial preoperative haemaglobin decline in hip fracture patients: a propensity-matched analysis of 1935 cases.
articles.authors: Graif N, Abdel Latif A, Warschawski Y, Elbaz E, Ben-Tov T, Snir N, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study with propensity score matching
articles.population: Hip fracture patients treated at a tertiary centre
articles.outcomes: Preoperative haemoglobin decline of 2 g/dL or more; transfusion rates; hospital length of stay
articles.take_home: About half of hip fracture patients lose a significant amount of blood before surgery, particularly those with extracapsular fractures, those over 85 years old, and those waiting more than 24 hours for surgery. Patients with substantial preoperative blood loss needed more transfusions and stayed in hospital longer.

articles.title: Early migration following revision total knee arthroplasty with tibial metaphyseal cones: a 2-year prospective RSA cohort study of 25 patients.
articles.authors: Mills K, Belt M, Goosen JHM, Van Hellemondt GG, Heesterbeek PJC
articles.study_type: Prospective cohort study using radiostereometric analysis
articles.population: Patients undergoing revision total knee replacement with tibial metaphyseal cones
articles.outcomes: Tibial component movement (translation and rotation) measured at 2 years; pain, satisfaction, Oxford Knee Score, Knee Society Score, KOOS-PS; adverse events including re-revision
articles.take_home: Most tibial cones remained stable with very small movement at 2 years, but there was considerable variation between patients with some showing more movement than others. Two patients needed further surgery within 2 years.

articles.title: 6-week cryo-compression therapy and opioid use after unicompartmental knee arthroplasty with comparable pain scores: a secondary analysis of a randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Biemans H, De Vries AJ, Brouwer RW
articles.population: Adults undergoing unicompartmental knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Pain at rest and during loading, oxycodone consumption, knee function scores, quality of life, physical tests
articles.take_home: Six weeks of cryo-compression therapy after knee replacement did not reduce pain compared to standard care, although patients using cryo-compression took about 46% less opioid painkillers. Other measures of recovery and quality of life were similar between groups.

articles.title: Validation of periprosthetic joint infection after total knee arthroplasty: a cohort study on 423 cases from the Norwegian Arthroplasty Register.
articles.authors: Mossige M, Lutro O, Fenstad AM, Leta TH, Bruun T, Furnes O, et al.
articles.population: Patients who had reoperation after total knee replacement in Western Norway
articles.outcomes: Sensitivity 92%, specificity 96%, and accuracy 94% for periprosthetic joint infection reporting; 94% of reported infections confirmed; 5% of non-infection cases validated as infections
articles.take_home: Norwegian surgeons are accurately reporting which knee replacement reoperations are due to infection, with 9 in 10 cases correctly identified. This means data from the Norwegian registry can be reliably used to track and study knee replacement infections.

articles.title: External validation of machine learning models for estimation of mortality 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after hip fracture on 5,055 consecutive patients.
articles.authors: Mosfeldt M, Jorgensen HL, Lauritzen JB, Jansson KA
articles.study_type: External validation study of machine learning models
articles.population: Patients with hip fractures treated at two hospitals in Stockholm, Sweden
articles.outcomes: Mortality at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after hip fracture
articles.take_home: Three types of computer models were tested to predict how many hip fracture patients would die within a year. The eXtreme Gradient Boosting model performed best and showed acceptable accuracy. After adjusting for differences between patient groups, the models worked well and are now available online to help doctors predict individual patient outcomes.

articles.title: CT-based micromotion analysis for tracking fracture displacement and fragment movement in trochanteric hip fractures: an agreement study.
articles.authors: Aberg H, Hanni M, Sandberg O, Jonsson KB
articles.population: Patients with trochanteric hip fractures treated with cephalomedullary nail
articles.outcomes: Measurement agreement of CT-based micromotion analysis for quantifying fracture displacement, surgical reduction quality, and postoperative fragment movement; repeatability assessed using repositioned follow-up scans
articles.take_home: CT-based micromotion analysis can precisely measure hip fracture displacement and healing with sub-millimetric accuracy, showing that fractures improved significantly after surgery and remained stable during healing. The technique reliably tracks small movements of bone fragments in three dimensions.

articles.title: How Do Operating Room Timings Differ Between Anterior and Posterior Approaches for Total Hip Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Ruff G, Di Pauli von Treuheim T, Sarfraz A, Metko K, Rozell JC, Schwarzkopf R, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary, unilateral, elective total hip arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Operating room timings including patient positioning, device implantation, wound closure, and total operating room time between anterior and posterior approaches
articles.take_home: Anterior approach hip replacement surgery takes about 10 minutes less total operating room time than posterior approach, mainly due to faster set-up, closure and take-down. However, if cement is used to fix the implant, the anterior approach takes considerably longer than the posterior approach.

articles.title: Factor XI and XIa Inhibitors for Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis After Total Knee Arthroplasty: A Review From Mechanism to Phase III Trials.
articles.authors: Smitterberg CW, Ando W, Chen AF, Garvin KL, Hamilton WG, Lee GC, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Venous thromboembolism prevention, bleeding risk, wound complications, infection risk
articles.take_home: Factor XI and FXIa inhibitors show promise as alternatives to current blood-thinning medicines after knee replacement, potentially reducing blood clots whilst causing less bleeding. Two large Phase III trials are underway to test whether these new treatments work better than existing options.

articles.title: Intraosseous Sustained-Release Anesthetics Promote Early Ambulation and Functional Recovery after Total Knee Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Huang J, Abdullah Ezzi SH, Wu S, Ali Alshabi MM, Zhang W, Adil MM, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee replacement, mean age 75 years
articles.outcomes: Pain levels at rest and during walking, analgesia requirements, walking distance, and knee bending and movement at days 1 and 3 after surgery
articles.take_home: Adding intraosseous sustained-release anesthetic injection into the bone during knee replacement surgery reduced pain and increased the distance patients could walk and how much they could bend their knee in the first three days after surgery compared to standard local anesthetic infiltration alone.

articles.title: Comparing Clinical and Radiological Outcomes for a Three-Dimensional Printed Highly-Porous Cup and a Hydroxyapatite-Coated Non-Highly-Porous Cup in Total Hip Arthroplasty Using Propensity Score Matching.
articles.authors: Okazaki T, Imagama T, Matsuki Y, Kaneoka T, Kawakami T, Sakai T
articles.study_type: Retrospective comparative study with propensity score matching
articles.population: Patients undergoing total hip arthroplasty with CT-based navigation
articles.sample_size: 132 hips (66 in each group after matching)
articles.outcomes: Clinical outcomes using JOA and JHEQ scores; radiological outcomes including cup orientation, migration, loosening, and radiolucent lines at two-year follow-up
articles.take_home: Three-dimensional printed highly porous cups and traditional hydroxyapatite-coated cups performed similarly after two years, with comparable clinical results and good long-term stability. Highly porous cups had more initial gaps between cup and bone, but these did not affect outcomes.

articles.title: Aspirin is Associated with Improved Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis Compared with Anticoagulants After Total Hip Arthroplasty in Patients Who Have Prior Hematologic Malignancy.
articles.authors: Paul BR, Verhey JT, Tarabichi S, Van Schuyver PR, Christopher ZK, Bingham JS, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using national administrative database
articles.population: Patients with prior hematologic malignancy who underwent total hip arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 1,043 patients receiving aspirin matched to 1,484 patients receiving non-aspirin anticoagulation
articles.outcomes: Primary: 90-day venous thromboembolism (blood clots). Secondary: emergency department visits, hospital readmissions, blood transfusions, pneumonia, and mortality
articles.take_home: Aspirin was associated with lower rates of blood clots in the legs and fewer hospital readmissions compared with stronger anticoagulants in patients with previous blood cancer undergoing hip replacement. Aspirin patients also needed fewer blood transfusions and had lower rates of pneumonia.

articles.title: Adequate canal-filling ratio and extension stem length reduce radiographic loosening in revision total knee arthroplasty using hybrid fixation with grit-blasted, fluted stems.
articles.authors: Shao PW, Lin YC, Chu CT, Chang CH, Hu CC, Lu TW, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing revision total knee replacement with minor bone defects, hybrid fixation and follow-up of at least 24 months
articles.outcomes: Radiographic loosening of the prosthesis, defined using the modified Knee Society scoring system
articles.take_home: The study found that using extension stems with adequate fill of the knee canal and sufficient length reduced the risk of prosthesis loosening after revision knee replacement. Surgeons should aim for femoral stem measurements of at least 0.83 fill ratio and 20.7mm length, and tibial stem measurements of at least 0.77 fill ratio and 18.7mm length.

articles.title: Intraoperative hypotension and short-term outcomes after primary total hip arthroplasty : a retrospective cohort study.
articles.authors: Burbelo A, Clark T, Huffman AB, Scarl N, O'Palko S, Ferguson B, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: 30-day and 90-day readmissions, postoperative length of stay, reoperation within 90 days
articles.take_home: Low blood pressure during hip replacement surgery (defined as a fixed threshold) was not linked to worse short-term outcomes like hospital readmission or longer stays. However, a relative drop in blood pressure during surgery was associated with higher rates of reoperation within 90 days, and greater blood loss during surgery was linked to longer hospital stays.

articles.title: Outcomes of Total Hip Arthroplasty Based on Surgeon Fellowship and Case Volume.
articles.authors: Katanbaf R, Misch M, Aflatooni JO, Smitterberg CW, Griffin D, Nace J, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using national all-payer database
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip replacement between 2010 and 2021
articles.outcomes: Hip dislocation, aseptic loosening, periprosthetic fracture, periprosthetic joint infection, surgical site infection, aseptic revision, and venous thromboembolism up to two years after surgery
articles.take_home: Surgeons who had specialised hip and knee fellowship training and performed high numbers of hip replacements had better results, particularly lower dislocation and infection rates. Even non-specialist surgeons with high case volumes did not achieve the same outcomes as specialist surgeons.

articles.title: Functional Outcomes, Complications, and Survivorship After Simultaneous Bilateral Total Hip Arthroplasty: A Retrospective Cohort Study with a Median Follow-up of 4.6 Years.
articles.authors: Diaz Dilernia F, Vasarhelyi E, Lanting B, Naudie D, McCalden R, MacDonald S, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing elective simultaneous bilateral total hip arthroplasty for end-stage bilateral hip disease
articles.outcomes: Functional scores, pain, complications, readmissions, implant survivorship. Overall complication rate 6.5%, transfusion required in 6.5% of patients, implant survivorship 95.7% at median 4.6 years follow-up
articles.take_home: Simultaneous bilateral hip replacement provides significant improvement in function and pain with good implant survival rates and acceptable complication rates when patients are carefully selected and modern surgical protocols are used.

articles.title: Is a 32 mm glenosphere optimal in reverse shoulder arthroplasty for patients with small glenoid anatomy? : a range of motion simulation study in petite Asian females.
articles.authors: Kiyomatsu H, Fujibuchi T, Kutsuna T, Kinoshita T, Tsuda T, Hayashi M, et al.
articles.study_type: Computational simulation study with retrospective outcomes review
articles.population: Asian female patients with small glenoid anatomy undergoing reverse shoulder arthroplasty, mean age 75.5 years
articles.sample_size: n = 11 for postoperative outcomes; unclear total number for simulations
articles.outcomes: Impingement-free range of motion in extension-flexion, abduction-adduction, and external-internal rotation with different glenosphere sizes (32 mm, 36 mm, 39 mm) and inferior overhangs (1.5 mm, 3.0 mm, 4.5 mm)
articles.take_home: In patients with small shoulder sockets, using a smaller 32 mm glenosphere with adequate inferior overhang of 3 mm or more predicted better movement without bone rubbing compared to larger sizes. The study used computer modelling and needs real-world clinical testing to confirm these findings account for soft tissues and implant stability.

articles.title: Minimal Movement Restrictions Do Not Increase Hip Dislocations Following Total Hip Arthroplasty: A Before-and-After Study of 10,357 Patients.
articles.authors: Klaassen AD, Willigenburg NW, Musters JWQ, Jager J, Poolman RW
articles.population: Patients undergoing total hip arthroplasty between January 2015 and March 2020
articles.sample_size: 10,357 patients (7,666 with strict restrictions, 2,691 with minimal restrictions)
articles.outcomes: Hip dislocation within 90 days after surgery
articles.take_home: Patients who followed a protocol with minimal movement restrictions after hip replacement did not have more hip dislocations than those with strict restrictions. This suggests that limiting restrictions after hip replacement surgery may be safe and could help patients return to normal activities more quickly.
